steps.title: Fastest path: mark the figure first
steps.content: Mark the given angle relationships on the figure first: equal angles, vertical angles, supplementary angles, parallel-line angles, and triangle sums of $180^\circ$. Most of these questions become a short angle chase once the givens are marked.

steps.title: Step 1
steps.content: In any triangle the three interior angles sum to 180°. For triangle ABC: $m\angle C = 180^\circ - 46^\circ - 109^\circ = 25^\circ$.

steps.title: Step 2
steps.content: For triangle JKL: $m\angle L = 180^\circ - 46^\circ - 109^\circ = 25^\circ$. Both triangles have the exact same three angles: 46°, 109°, and 25°.

steps.title: Step 3
steps.content: The Angle-Angle (AA) similarity criterion states that if two pairs of corresponding angles are equal, the triangles are similar. Here $\angle A = \angle J = 46^\circ$ and $\angle B = \angle K = 109^\circ$, so similarity is already established from the given information alone.

choices.explanation: • Angle C is fully determined by the angle-sum property: $180^\circ - 46^\circ - 109^\circ = 25^\circ$. It provides no new information beyond what is already given. This is not needed.
• Angle L is likewise fixed at $180^\circ - 46^\circ - 109^\circ = 25^\circ$. Learning it explicitly changes nothing about the similarity conclusion. This is not needed.

choices.text: The measure of angle C and the measure of angle L
choices.explanation: • Both angles are already determined by arithmetic from the given information. Knowing both still adds nothing beyond what can be computed directly. This is not needed.
• With $\angle A = \angle J = 46^\circ$ and $\angle B = \angle K = 109^\circ$, AA similarity is satisfied immediately. The third angles are forced to equal 25° each. That confirms D is correct.
